Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only defines variables and arrays at the top level. The source URLs point to the official GitHub releases of the PostHog CLI, with pinned checksums. There is no `pkgver()` function, and no dangerous commands (curl, wget, eval, etc.) in the global scope. The `package()` function is not executed during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. Therefore, running these commands is safe.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code in global scope; safe to fetch sources.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code in global scope; safe to fetch sources.[/]

[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD follows standard packaging practices for a binary AUR package. It downloads a pre-compiled tarball from the official GitHub releases of the PostHog project (`https://github.com/PostHog/posthog`), uses pinned SHA256 checksums, and installs the binary and license file into the package directory. No suspicious commands (curl, wget, eval, base64), obfuscation, exfiltration, or tampering with system files is present. The source URLs are consistent with the package's declared upstream, and the checksums are not skipped, which provides integrity verification. There are no deviations from expected behavior for a binary CLI package.
</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD with pinned checksums from official source.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for CHANGELOG.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard CHANGELOG.md documenting the version history and changes for the `posthog-cli` project. It contains only human-readable text: version numbers, dates, commit hashes with links to the upstream GitHub repository, and descriptions of features, fixes, and improvements. There are no executable instructions, no embedded scripts, no network requests, no obfuscated code, and no attempts to modify system files or exfiltrate data. The references to "Compromised" in version 0.5.15 are the author's own acknowledgment of a past supply-chain incident in a previous release, not malicious content in this file. This is purely a changelog and poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>Standard changelog with no executable content.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[3/4] Reviewing src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/lib/posthog-api-cli.mjs...
+ Reviewed src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/CHANGELOG.md. Status: SAFE -- Standard changelog with no executable content.
LLM auditresponse for posthog-api-cli.mjs:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a bundled Node.js application that serves as the CLI entry point for the PostHog API tool. It imports common libraries (yaml, zod, @posthog/core) and defines handlers for various PostHog API operations. The suspicious pattern matches (base64, eval, etc.) are benign:

- Base64 references come from the zod validation library (which validates base64-encoded strings for MCP data) and from YAML base64 parsing. These are standard library functions, not obfuscated payloads.
- The `eval` match is inside a regex comment in a stack trace parser (Gecko stack parsing), not an actual invocation of `eval()`.
- No `curl`, `wget`, or `exec` calls are present. The code makes HTTP requests only through `context.api.request()` which targets PostHog's own API endpoints (e.g., `/api/projects/...`), consistent with the application's purpose.

The file is compiled/bundled with standard tooling (esbuild or similar) and contains no injected malicious code. It operates entirely within the expected scope of a PostHog CLI tool.
</details>
<summary>Standard bundled Node.js application, no malicious code found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/posthog-cli-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-79hhbpv8...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-79hhbpv8
Analyzing posthog-cli-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-79hhbpv8) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard source URLs that point directly to official release binaries hosted on GitHub at `github.com/PostHog/posthog`, which is consistent with the declared upstream. The sources are arch-specific tarballs containing prebuilt binaries, and the URLs use HTTPS from a trusted domain. The `sha256sums` are provided and pinned for both architectures, so integrity is verified upon download.

There is no top-level command substitution, `pkgver()` function override, or executable code outside of the `package()` function. The variables are simple string assignments using static values or predictable expansions (e.g., `_base_url` from `pkgver`). Since `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare --printsrcinfo` only sources the PKGBUILD and downloads the sources without entering `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()`, no potentially harmful code execution occurs during this phase. All operations are limited to secure, declarative sourcing and fetching.

Thus, executing the requested `makepkg` commands does not trigger any dynamic or malicious behavior such as remote code execution, data exfiltration, or unauthorized network access beyond downloading the declared sources.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous code in sourcing or source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous code in sourcing or source fetch[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD file defines a standard binary package build process for the PostHog CLI tool. It fetches pre-built binaries from the official GitHub releases of the PostHog project, specifically from a URL that matches the declared `url` field (`https://github.com/PostHog/posthog`). The sources are properly scoped to architecture-specific tarballs for x86_64 and aarch64, and SHA-256 checksums are provided and verified for both architectures, ensuring integrity.

The package function uses `install` to copy the binary to `/usr/bin/posthog-cli` and the license file to the appropriate location under `/usr/share/licenses`, which follows standard Arch packaging practices. There are no attempts to download additional files at build or install time, no use of `eval`, `base64`, `curl | bash`, or other dangerous patterns. All operations are transparent and confined to installing the provided upstream binary and its license.

The dependencies and provides/conflicts are reasonable for a standalone CLI tool. The use of a binary-only package (bin) is common in AUR for tools not easily built from source. No obfuscated code, unexpected network activity, or system modifications are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with verified checksums, no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard LICENSE file declaring the copyright and licensing terms for the posthog-cli-bin package. It states that most content is licensed under the MIT Expat license, with exceptions for content in the "ee/" directory and third-party components, which follow their respective licenses. There is no executable code, no network activity, no file manipulation, and no potentially dangerous commands present. The content is purely legal text and poses no security risk. The presence of such a license file in an AUR package is normal and expected.

No obfuscated content, suspicious instructions, or malicious patterns are present. The file does not perform any actions, nor does it contain scripts or commands that could be exploited in a supply chain attack. It complies with standard open source licensing practices.
</details>
<summary>
License file with no executable or malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/4] Reviewing src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/CHANGELOG.md, src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/README.md, src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/lib/posthog-api-cli.mjs...
+ Reviewed src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/LICENSE. Status: SAFE -- License file with no executable or malicious content
LLM auditresponse for CHANGELOG.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a `CHANGELOG.md`, which is a standard plain-text log of changes between software versions. It contains no executable code, scripts, or embedded commands of any kind. The content consists entirely of version numbers, dates, commit references, and human-readable descriptions of updates, such as bug fixes, feature additions, and improvements related to the posthog-cli tool.

Notably, while version 0.5.16 references "fixing compromised package" and version 0.5.15 simply states "Compromised", these are acknowledgments of a past security incident in earlier versions, not indications of current malicious content. The changelog itself does not contain any payloads, obfuscation, suspicious URLs, or commands that could lead to data exfiltration or remote execution.

As this file serves only documentation purposes and poses no inherent security risk, it is considered SAFE.
</details>
<summary>
Changelog only; no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/4] Reviewing src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/README.md, src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/lib/posthog-api-cli.mjs...
+ Reviewed src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/CHANGELOG.md. Status: SAFE -- Changelog only; no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for README.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `README.md` file contains documentation for the Posthog CLI tool, describing its commands, authentication methods, environment variables, and use cases. It explains supported workflows such as interactive login, CI/CD integration via environment variables, dry-run mode for testing, and agent-first API tooling. The content describes legitimate application functionality and standard security practices like scope-limited API keys and precedence rules for configuration sources.

There is no executable code, scripts, or commands in the file that perform system operations, network requests, or file manipulations. The examples shown (e.g., `posthog-cli api agents-md install`) describe intended usage of the CLI and do not indicate malicious behavior. References to configuration files like `~/.posthog/credentials.json` or `.env` are within the expected scope of CLI tool operation and do not imply data exfiltration.

While the CLI interacts with PostHog services and supports automation for agents, these capabilities are clearly part of the tool's designed purpose. No obfuscated commands, unauthorized data access, or supply-chain attack vectors are present or suggested in the documentation.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation only; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[3/4] Reviewing src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/lib/posthog-api-cli.mjs...
+ Reviewed src/posthog-cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/README.md. Status: SAFE -- Documentation only; no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for posthog-api-cli.mjs:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file `posthog-api-cli.mjs` is a Node.js script implementing a command-line interface for the PostHog API, primarily used to execute various tools (like querying insights, managing projects, workflows, etc.) based on user commands.

The file consists mostly of dependency imports and code definitions (using CommonJS patterns) from internal `node_modules`. The actual executable logic is contained in a standard CLI `main()` function at the end. It parses `process.argv`, handles commands like `tools`, `search`, `call`, and executes corresponding actions.

The numerous suspicious pattern matches relate to base64 encoding/decoding and validation (e.g., in zod schema definitions and a base64 resolver) — a common and legitimate practice for validating data, especially when handling binary data in JSON (like images, audio) or transmission. Finds of `eval` are within regular expression *literals* (`chromeEvalRegex`) used to parse stack traces, not code execution. The file does not perform any network requests outside its core API functions, does not spawn child processes, and doesn't interact with sensitive system files.

All detected patterns are consistent with standard library functions for data validation and error handling in a well-maintained open-source project.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious or suspicious activity detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[4/4] Reviewing ...
